title: Fugitive Freedom and Radical Care: Towards a Standpoint Theory of Normativity
author: Loick

opening_move.description: Opening with Césaire's 'boomerang' metaphor for the moral corruption that domination inflicts on the dominator, the paper diagnoses a gap in standpoint theory: while epistemic standpoint theories are well-developed (Mills, Fricker), there is no parallel standpoint theory of normativity. The puzzle: if domination distorts not only epistemic but also normative capacities, oppressed groups may have normative advantages — but this has not been theorized.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A normative standpoint theory grounded in two heterogeneous traditions — the Black Radical Tradition (fugitive freedom) and feminist care ethics — can provide a theory of how political struggle enables oppressed groups to develop superior normative orientations.

rivals_engaged: universalist ethics that ignore social situatedness
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper extends and synthesizes. It extends epistemic standpoint theory (Mills' 'moral ignorance' thesis) into a normative domain. It synthesizes two traditions — the Black Radical Tradition on fugitive freedom (Harriet Tubman, Frederick Douglass, Angela Davis) and feminist care ethics (Gilligan, Noddings, Held) — arguing both are grounded in similar presuppositions (political struggle provoked by social contradictions). The paper builds a framework by identifying three aspects: passive conditions enabling normative advantage; active development through struggle; translation into emancipatory practice.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Conceptual-genealogical: reads key texts of the Black Radical Tradition (Tubman, Douglass, Davis) and feminist care ethics as philosophical sources for theorizing normative standpoints. Uses Hegel's master-servant dialectic as structural resource.
epistemic_mode.notes: The paper engages historical figures and practices (Harriet Tubman's Underground Railroad, feminist care theory) not as empirical cases but as philosophical resources for conceptual construction. This is fully_normative because the argument moves entirely within conceptual and normative space — the examples are philosophical touchstones, not empirical evidence.

normative_stake.domain: normative theory / standpoint theory / critical theory / abolition / care ethics

confidence_notes: Classified fully_normative because the references to Tubman, Douglass, and care practice are conceptual resources for framework construction, not empirical cases. The move_sequence includes case_introduction/case_development because these traditions are introduced and developed as philosophical materials — analogous to how other fully_normative papers use thinkers as conceptual resources. The paper is a Type 2b variant (reconstruction of two traditions into a new synthesis) but with a novel architecture: synthesizing Black Radical Tradition + feminist care ethics into a standpoint theory of normativity. This makes it distinctive within the PSC corpus.
